text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Amelia Shah
Riverside Health Centre

12 Brookfield Road
Lewisham
London SE13 7QP
Dear Dr Amelia Shah
Sophie Turner, DOB: 14-03-1990, Hospital No: H739526 NHS No. 8426619053
21 Meadow Court, Deptford, London, SE8 3JL 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, probable temporal lobe onset; history of migraine without aura

MRI 2019: structurally normal brain; no clear epileptogenic lesion identified
EEG 2020 and 2023: intermittent left temporal sharp activity; no secondary generalisation captured

Previous medication: lamotrigine (partial benefit), levetiracetam (mood-related adverse effects), carbamazepine (sedation), propranolol for migraine prophylaxis (historical)

Not taken: lacosamide, zonisamide, topiramate, perampanel

I reviewed Sophie Turner in clinic today, accompanied by her partner. She reports improved day-to-day functioning and has maintained employment. Headaches consistent with her background migraine occur about twice per month and respond to naproxen. With regard to her epilepsy, episodes over the past three months have largely represented dissociative events rather than epileptic attacks; in other words, recent spells have not been epileptic in nature based on description and collateral, and there have been no witnessed focal impaired-awareness seizures during this interval. She last had a clearly epileptic focal event approximately six months ago, characterised by déjà vu, rising epigastric sensation, brief speech arrest, and post-event fatigue without convulsion.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
- Naproxen 500 mg as needed for migraine (no more than 2–3 days/week)
- Omeprazole 20 mg once daily for gastric protection when using NSAIDs

Allergies: no known drug allergies

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; no recent epileptic seizures to mandate change. Adherence discussed and confirmed.
- Provide seizure and event diary template to differentiate probable non-epileptic episodes from epileptic events; partner to document semiology and duration when possible.
- Refer to our epilepsy nurse specialist for guidance on managing functional/dissociative episodes and signposting to local psychology services if episodes persist.
- Safety reviewed: no driving as per DVLA regulations until criteria met; patient verbalised understanding. First aid and SUDEP information reinforced.
- Migraine: continue current approach; if frequency increases, consider preventive options via GP or at next review.
- Routine follow-up in 6 months, sooner if events change character, increase in frequency, or if any new neurological symptoms arise.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: there have been no witnessed focal impaired-awareness seizures during this interval. She last had a clearly epileptic focal event approximately six months ago

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter specifies that over the past three months, recent episodes were dissociative and there were no witnessed focal impaired-awareness seizures during this interval. It further states the last clearly epileptic focal event was approximately six months ago. To be considered seizure free, the patient must have no seizures for at least 6 months. The wording says 'approximately six months ago,' which is borderline but suggests around six months without epileptic seizures. There are no counts of epileptic seizures within the last three months (zero), and only a single clearly epileptic event about six months prior. Given the uncertainty around 'approximately,' we cannot definitively confirm a full six months; per the rules, if insufficient to confirm the 6‑month criterion, classify as 'seizure free for multiple months.'
seizure_frequency_number: there have been no witnessed focal impaired-awareness seizures during this interval. She last had a clearly epileptic focal event approximately six months ago
